author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XVII. 10.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And His disciples asked Him, saying, Why then say the Scribes that Elias must first come?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not then from the Scriptures did they know this, but the Scribes used to explain themselves, and this saying was reported abroad amongst the ignorant people; as about Christ also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore the Samaritan woman also said, “Messiah cometh; when He is come, He will tell us all things:”and they themselves asked John, “Art thou Elias, or the Prophet?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the saying, as I said, prevailed, both that concerning the Christ and that concerning Elias, not however rightly interpreted by them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the Scriptures speak of two advents of Christ, both this that is past, and that which is to come; and declaring these Paul said, “The grace of God, that bringeth salvation, hath appeared, teaching us, that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, and righteously, and godly.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Behold the one, hear how he declares the other also; for having said these things, he added, “Looking for the blessed hope and appearing of our great God and Saviour Jesus Christ.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the prophets too mention both; of the one, however, that is, of the second, they say Elias will be the forerunner.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For of the first, John was forerunner; whom Christ called also Elias, not because he was Elias, but because he was fulfilling the ministry of that prophet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as the one shall be forerunner of the second advent, so was the other too of the first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the Scribes, confusing these things and perverting the people, made mention of that other only to the people, the second advent, and said, “If this man is the Christ, Elias ought to have come beforehand.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore the disciples too speak as follows, “How then say the Scribes, Elias must first come?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore also the Pharisees sent unto John, and asked him, “Art thou Elias?”making no mention anywhere of the former advent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is the solution, which Christ alleged?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Elias indeed cometh then, before my second advent; and now too is Elias come;” so calling John.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In this sense Elias is come: but if thou wouldest seek the Tishbite, he is coming.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He said, “Elias truly cometh, and shall restore all things.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All what things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Such as the Prophet Malachi spake of; for “I will send you,” saith He, “Elias the Tishbite, who shall restore the heart of father to son, lest I come and utterly smite the earth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the accuracy of prophetical language? how, because Christ called John, Elias, by reasoning of their community of office, lest thou shouldest suppose this to be the meaning of the prophet too in this place, He added His country also, saying, “the Tishbite;”whereas John was not a Tishbite.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And herewith He sets down another sign also, saying, “Lest I come and utterly smite the earth,” signifying His second and dreadful advent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in the first He came not to smite the earth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, “I came not,” saith He, “to judge the world, but to save the world.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To show therefore that the Tishbite comes before that other advent, which hath the judgment, He said this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the reason too of his coming He teaches withal.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is this reason?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That when He is come, he may persuade the Jews to believe in Christ, and that they may not all utterly perish at His coming.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He too, guiding them on to that remembrance, saith, “And he shall restore all things;” that is, shall correct the unbelief of the Jews that are then in being.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hence the extreme accuracy of his expression; in that he said not, “He will restore the heart of the son to the father,” but “of the father to the son.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the Jews being fathers of the apostles, his meaning is, that he will restore to the doctrines of their sons, that is, of the apostles, the hearts of the fathers, that is, the Jewish people’s mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But I say unto you, that Elias is come already, and they knew him not, but have done unto him whatsoever they listed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Likewise shall also the Son of Man suffer of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then they understood that He spake to them of John.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet neither the Scribes said this, nor the Scriptures; but because now they were sharper and more attentive to His sayings, they quickly caught His meaning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whence did the disciples know this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He had already told them, “He is Elias, which was for to come;”but here, that he hath come; and again, that “Elias cometh and will restore all things.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But be not thou troubled, nor imagine that His statement wavers, though at one time He said, “he will come,” at another, “he hath come.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For all these things are true.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since when He saith, “Elias indeed cometh, and will restore all things,” He means Elias himself, and the conversion of the Jews which is then to take place; but when He saith, “Which was for to come,” He calls John, Elias, with regard to the manner of his administration.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and so the prophets used to call every one of their approved kings, David;and the Jews, “rulers of Sodom,”and “sons of Ethiopians;”because of their ways.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as the other shall be forerunner of the second advent, so was this of the first.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not for this only doth He call him Elias everywhere, but to signify His perfect agreement with the Old Testament, and that this advent too is according to prophecy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also He adds again, “He came, and they knew him not, but have done unto him all things whatsoever they listed.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What means, “call things whatsoever they listed?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They cast him into prison, they used him despitefully, they slew him, they brought his head in a charger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Likewise shall also the Son of Man suffer of them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how again He in due season reminds them of His passion, laying up for them great store of comfort from the passion of John.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not in this way only, but also by presently working great miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and whensoever He speaks of His passion, presently He works miracles, both after those sayings and before them; and in many places one may find Him to have kept this rule.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then,” for instance, it saith, “He began to signify how that He must go unto Jerusalem, and be killed, and suffer many things.”“Then:” when? when He was confessed to be Christ, and the Son of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again on the mountain, when He had shown them the marvellous vision, and the prophets had been discoursing of His glory, He reminded them of His passion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For having spoken of the history concerning John, He added, “Likewise shall also the Son of Man suffer of them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And after a little while again, when He had cast out the devil, which His disciples were not able to cast out; for then too, “As they abode in Galilee,” so it saith, “Jesus said unto them, The Son of Man shall be betrayed into the hands of sinfulmen, and they shall kill Him, and the third day He shall rise again.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now in doing this, He by the greatness of the miracles was abating the excess of their sorrow, and in every way consoling them; even as here also, by the mention of John’s death, He afforded them much consolation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But should any one say, “Wherefore did He not even now raise up Elias and send him, witnessing as He doth so great good of his coming?” we should reply, that even as it was, while thinking Christ to be Elias, they did not believe Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “some say,” such are the words, “that Thou art Elias, and others, Jeremias.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And indeed between John and Elias, there was no difference but the time only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then how will they believe at that time?” it may be said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, “he will restore all things,” not simply by being recognized, but also because the glory of Christ will have been growing more intense up to that day, and will be among all clearer than the sun.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore, preceded by such an opinion and expectation, he comes making the same proclamation as John, and himself also announcing Jesus, they will more easily receive his sayings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But in saying, “They knew him not,” He is excusing also what was done in His own case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not in this way only doth He console them, but also by pointing out that John’s sufferings at their hands, whatever they are, are undeserved; and by His throwing into the shade what would annoy them, by means of two signs, the one on the mountain, the other just about to take place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when they heard these things, they do not ask Him when Elias cometh; being straitened either by grief at His passion, or by fear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For on many occasions, upon seeing Him unwilling to speak a thing clearly, they are silent, and so an end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For instance, when during their abode in Galilee He said, “The Son of Man shall be betrayed, and they shall kill Him;”it is added by Mark, “That they understood not the saying, and were afraid to ask Him;”by Luke, “That it was hid from them, that they might not perceive it, and they feared to ask Him of that saying.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And when they were come to the multitude, there came to Him a man, kneeling down to Him, and saying, Lord, have mercy on my son, for he is lunatic, and sore vexed;for ofttimes he falleth into the fire, and oft into the water.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And I brought him unto Thy disciples, and they could not cure him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This man the Scripture signifies to be exceedingly weak in faith; and this is many ways evident; from Christ’s saying, “All things are possible to him that believeth;”from the saying of the man himself that approached, “Help Thou mine unbelief:”from Christ’s commanding the devil to “enter no more into him;”and from the man’s saying again to Christ, “If Thou canst.”“Yet if his unbelief was the cause,” it may be said, “that the devil went not out, why doth He blame the disciples?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Signifying, that even without persons to bring the sick in faith, they might in many instances work a cure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as the faith of the person presenting oftentimes availed for receiving the cure, even from inferior ministers; so the power of the doers oftentimes sufficed, even without belief in those who came to work the miracle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And both these things are signified in the Scripture.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For both they of the company of Cornelius by their faith drew unto themselves the grace of the Spirit; and in the case of Eliseusagain, when none had believed, a dead man rose again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to those that cast him down, not for faith but for cowardice did they cast him, unintentionally and by chance, for fear of the band of robbers, and so they fled: while the person himself that was cast in was dead, yet by the mere virtue of the holy body the dead man arose.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence it is clear in this case, that even the disciples were weak; but not all; for the pillarswere not present there.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And see this man’s want of consideration, from another circumstance again, how before the multitude he pleads to Jesus against His disciples, saying, “I brought him to Thy disciples, and they could not cure him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He, acquitting them of the charges before the people, imputes the greater part to him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, “O faithless and perverse generation,” these are His words, “how long shall I be with you?”not aiming at his person only, lest He should confound the man, but also at all the Jews.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed many of those present might probably be offended, and have undue thoughts of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when He said, “How long shall I be with you,” He indicates again death to be welcome to Him, and the thing an object of desire, and His departure longed for, and that not crucifixion, but being with them, is grievous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He stopped not however at the accusations; but what saith He?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Bring him hither to me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Himself moreover asks him, “how long time he is thus;” both making a plea for His disciples, and leading the other to a good hope, and that he might believe in his attaining deliverance from the evil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He suffers him to be torn, not for display (accordingly, when a crowd began to gather, He proceeded to rebuke him), but for the father’s own sake, that when he should see the evil spirit disturbed at Christ’s mere call, so at least, if in no other way, he might be led to believe the coming miracle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And because he had said, “Of a child,” and, “If thou canst help me,” Christ saith, “To him that believeth, all things are possible,”again giving the complaint a turn against him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas when the leper said, “If Thou wilt, Thou canst make me clean,”bearing witness to His authority Christ commending him, and confirming His words, said, “I will, be thou clean;” in this man’s case, upon his uttering a speech in no way worthy of His power,—“If Thou canst, help me,”—see how He corrects it, as not rightly spoken.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what saith He?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What He saith is like this: “Such abundance of power is with me, that I can even make others work these miracles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that if thou believe as one ought, even thou thyself art able,” saith He, “to heal both this one, and many others.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And having thus said, He set free the possessed of the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do thou not only from this observe His providence and His beneficence, but also from that other time, during which He allowed the devil to be in him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since surely, unless the man had been favored with much providential care even then, he would have perished long ago; for “it cast him both into the fire,” so it is said, “and into the water.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he that dared this would assuredly have destroyed the man too, unless even in so great madness God had put on him His strong curb: as indeed was the case with those naked men that were running in the deserts and cutting themselves with stones.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if he call him “a lunatic,” trouble not thyself at all, for it is the father of the possessed who speaks the word.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then saith the evangelist also, “He healed many that were lunatic?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Denominating them according to the impression of the multitude.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the evil spirit, to bring a reproach upon nature,by wine?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the weaker the vessel, the more entire the shipwreck, whether she be free or a slave.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the free woman behaves herself unseemly in the midst of her slaves as spectators, and the slave again in like manner in the midst of the slaves, and they cause the gifts of God to be blasphemously spoken of by foolish men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For instance, I hear many say, when these excesses happen, “Would there were no wine.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When other men sin, dost thou find fault with God’s gifts?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what great madness is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What? did the wine, O man, produce this evil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not the wine, but the intemperance of such as take an evil delight in it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Say then, “Would there were no drunkenness, no luxury;” but if thou say, “Would there were no wine,” thou wilt say, going on by degrees, “Would there were no steel, because of the murderers; no night, because of the thieves; no light, because of the informers; no women, because of adulteries;” and, in a word, thou wilt destroy all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do not so; for this is of a satanical mind; do not find fault with the wine, but with the drunkenness; and when thou hast found this self-same man sober, sketch out all his unseemliness, and say unto him, Wine was given, that we might be cheerful, not that we might behave ourselves unseemly; that we might laugh, not that we might be a laughingstock; that we might be healthful, not that we might be diseased; that we might correct the weakness of our body, not cast down the might of our soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: God honored thee with the gift, why disgrace thyself with the excess thereof?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear what Paul saith, “Use a little wine for thy stomach’s sake, and thine often infirmities.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if that saint, even when oppressed with disease, and enduring successive sicknesses, partook not of wine, until his Teacher suffered him; what excuse shall we have, who are drunken in health?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To him indeed He said, “Use a little wine for thy stomach’s sake;” but to each of you who are drunken, He will say, “Use little wine, for thy fornications, thy frequent filthy talking, for the other wicked desires to which drunkenness is wont to give birth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if ye are not willing, for these reasons, to abstain; at least on account of the despondencies which come of it, and the vexations, do ye abstain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wine was given for gladness, “Yea, wine,” so it is said, “maketh glad the heart of man:”but ye mar even this excellence in it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what kind of gladness is it to be beside one’s self, and to have innumerable vexations, and to see all things whirling round, and to be oppressed with giddiness, and like those that have a fever, to require some who may drench their heads with oil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These things are not said by me to all: or rather they are said to all, not because all are drunken, God forbid; but because they who do not drink take no thought of the drunken.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore even against you do I rather inveigh, that are in health; since the physician too leaves the sick, and addresses his discourse to them that are sitting by them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To you therefore do I direct my speech, entreating you neither to be at any time over-taken by this passion, and to draw upas by cords those who have been so overtaken, that they be not found worse than the brutes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they indeed seek nothing more than what is needful, but these have become even more brutish than they, overpassing the boundaries of moderation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For how much better is the ass than these men? how much better the dog!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed each of these animals, and of all others, whether it need to eat, or to drink, acknowledges sufficiency for a limit, and goes not on beyond what it needs; and though there are innumerable persons to constrain, it will not endure to go on to excess.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In this respect then we are worse even than the brutes, by the judgment not of them that are in health only, but even by our own.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that ye have judged yourselves to be baser than both dogs and asses,revealed to Peter, He doth hereby again confirm.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And neither at this did He stop, but by His very condescension declares this self-same truth; an instance of exceeding wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after thus speaking, He saith, “But lest we should offend them, go thou and cast an hook into the sea, and take up the fish that first cometh up, and thou shalt find therein a piece of money;that take, and give unto them for me and thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See how He neither declines the tribute, nor simply commands to pay it, but having first proved Himself not liable to it, then He gives it: the one to save the people, the other, those around Him, from offense.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He gives it not at all as a debt, but as doing the bestfor their weakness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Elsewhere, however, He despises the offense, when He was discoursing of meats,teaching us to know at what seasons we ought to consider them that are offended, and at what to disregard them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And indeed by the very mode of giving He discloses Himself again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wherefore doth He not command him to give of what they have laid up?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That, as I have said, herein also He might signify Himself to be God of all, and the sea also to be under His rule.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He had indeed signified this even already, by His rebuke, and by His commanding this same Peter to walk on the waves; but He now again signifies the self-same thing, though in another way, yet so as to cause herein great amazement.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither was it a small thing, to foretell that the first, who out of those depths should come in his way, would be the fish that would pay the tribute; and having cast forth His commandment like a net into that abyss, to bring up the one that bore the piece of money; but it was of a divine and unutterable power, thus to make even the sea bear gifts, and that its subjection to Him should be shown on all hands, as well when in its madness it was silent,and when, though fierce, it received its fellow servant;as now again, when it makes payment in His behalf to them that are demanding it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And give unto them,” He saith, “for me and thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the exceeding greatness of the honor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See also the self-command of Peter’s mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this point Mark, the follower of this apostle, doth not appear to have set down, because it indicated the great honor paid to him; but while of the denial he wrote as well as the rest, the things that make him illustrious he hath passed over in silence, his master perhaps entreating him not to mention the great things about himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He used the phrase, “for me and thee,” because Peter too was a firstborn child.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now as thou art amazed at Christ’s power, so I bid thee admire also the disciple’s faith, that to a thing beyond possibility he so gave ear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed it was very far beyond possibility by nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also in requital for his faith, He joined him to Himself in the payment of the tribute.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “In that hour came the disciples unto Jesus, saying, Who then is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The disciples experienced some feeling of human weakness; wherefore the evangelist also adds this note, saying, “In that hour;” when He had preferred him to all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For of James too, and John, one was a firstborn son, but no such thing as this had He done for them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, being ashamed to avow their feeling, they say not indeed openly, “Wherefore hast thou preferred Peter to us?” or, “Is he greater than we are?” for they were ashamed; but indefinitely they ask, “Who then is greater?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when they saw the three preferred, they felt nothing of the kind; but now that the honor had come round to one, they were vexed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not for this only, but there were many other things which they put together to kindle that feeling.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to him He had said, “I will give thee the keys;”to him, “Blessed art thou, Simon Barjona;” to him here, “Give unto them for me and thee;” and seeing too in general how freely he was allowed to speak, it somewhat fretted them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if Mark saith,that they did not ask, but reasoned in themselves, that is nothing contrary to this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is likely that they did both the one and the other, and whereas before, on another occasion, they had had this feeling, both once and twice, that now they did both declare it, and reason among themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But to thee I say, “Look not to the charge against them only, but consider this too; first, that they seek none of the things of this world; next, that even this passion they afterwards laid aside, and give up the first place one to another.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we are not able to attain so much as unto their faults, neither do we seek, “who is greatestin the kingdom of heaven;” but, who is greatestin the earthly kingdom, who is wealthiest, who most powerful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then saith Christ?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He unveils their conscience, and replies to their feeling, not merely to their words.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For He called a little child unto Him,” saith the Scripture, “and said, Except ye be converted, and become as this little child, ye shall not enter into the kingdom of heaven.”“Why, you,” He saith, “inquire who is greatest, and are contentious for first honors; but I pronounce him, that is not become lowest of all, unworthy so much as to enter in thither.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And full well doth He both allege that pattern, and not allege it only, but also set the child in the midst, by the very sight abashing them, and persuading them to be in like manner lowly and artless.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since both from envy the little child is pure, and from vainglory, and from longing for the first place; and he is possessed of the greatest of virtues, simplicity, and whatever is artless and lowly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not courage then only is wanted, nor wisdom, but this virtue also, humility I mean, and simplicity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and the things that belong to our salvation halt even in the chiefest point, if these be not with us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The little child, whether it be insulted and beaten, or honored and glorified, neither by the one is it moved to impatience or envy, nor by the other lifted up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how again He calls us on to all natural excellencies, indicating that of free choice it is possible to attain them, and so silences the wicked frenzy of the Manichæans?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if nature be an evil thing, wherefore doth He draw from hence His patterns of severe goodness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the child which He set in the midst I suppose to have been a very young child indeed, free from all these passions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such a little child is free from pride and the mad desire of glory, and envy, and contentiousness, and all such passions, and having many virtues, simplicity, humility, unworldliness,prides itself upon none of them; which is a twofold severity of goodness; to have these things, and not to be puffed up about them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He brought it in, and set it in the midst; and not at this merely did He conclude His discourse, but carries further this admonition, saying, “And whoso shall receive such a little child in my name, receiveth me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For know,” saith He, “that not only, if ye yourselves become like this, shall ye receive a great reward; but also if for my sake ye honor others who are such, even for your honor to them do I appoint unto you a kingdom as your recompence.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, He sets down what is far greater, saying, “he receiveth me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So exceedingly dear to me is all that is lowly and artless.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by “a little child,” here, He means the men that are thus simple and lowly, and abject and contemptible in the judgment of the common sort.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: After this, to obtain yet more acceptance for His saying, He establishes it not by the honor only, but also by the punishment, going on to say, “And whoso shall offend one of these little ones, it were better for him that a millstone were hanged about his neck, and that he were drowned in the depth of the sea.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For as they,” saith He, “who honor these for my sake, have heaven, or rather an honor greater than the very kingdom; even so they likewise who dishonor them (for this is to offend them), shall suffer the extremity of punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And marvel thou not at His calling the affront “an offense;”for many feeble-minded persons have suffered no ordinary offense from being treated with slight and insult.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To heighten therefore and aggravate the blame, He states the mischief arising therefrom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He doth not go on to express the punishment in the same way, but from the things familiar to us, He indicates how intolerable it is.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when He would touch the grosser sort most sharply, He brings sensible images.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore here also, meaning to indicate the greatness of the punishment they shall undergo, and to strike into the arrogance of those that despise them, He brought forward a kind of sensible punishment, that of the millstone, and of the drowning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet surely it were suitable to what had gone before to have said, “He that receiveth not one of these little ones, receiveth not me;” a thing bitterer than any punishment; but since the very unfeeling, and exceeding gross, were not so much penetrated by this, terrible as it is, He puts “a millstone,” and “a drowning.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He said not, “A millstone shall be hanged about his neck,” but, “It were better for him”to undergo this; implying that another evil, more grievous than this, awaits him; and if this be unbearable, much more that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how in both respects He made His threat terrible, first by the comparison with the known image rendering it more distinct, then by the excess on its side presenting it to the fancy as far greater than that visible one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how He plucks up by the root the spirit of arrogance; how He heals the ulcer of vainglory; how He instructs us in nothing to set our heart on the first honors; how He persuades such as covet them in everything to follow after the lowest place?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing is worse than arrogance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This even takes men out of their natural senses, and brings upon them the character of fools; or rather, it really makes them to be utterly like idiots.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For like as, if any one, being three cubits in stature, were to strive to be higher than the mountains, or actually to think it, and draw himself up, as overpassing their summits, we should seek no other proof of his being out of his senses; so also when thou seest a man arrogant, and thinking himself superior to all, and accounting it a degradation to live with other people, seek not thou after that to see any other proof of that man’s madness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, he is much more ridiculous than any natural fool, inasmuch as he absolutely creates this his disease on purpose.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not in this only is he wretched, but because he doth without feeling it fall into the very gulf of wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when will such an one come to due knowledge of any sin? when will he perceive that he is offending?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, rather he is as a vile and captive slave, whom the devil having caught goes off with, and makes him altogether a prey, buffetting him on every side, and encompassing him with ten thousand insults.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For unto such great folly doth he lead them in the end, as to get them to be haughty towards their children, and wives, and towards their own forefathers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And others, on the contrary, He causes to be puffed up by the distinction of their ancestors.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now, what can be more foolish than this? when from opposite causes people are alike puffed up, the one sort because they had mean persons for fathers, grandfathers, and ancestors; and the other because theirs were glorious and distinguished?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How then may one abate in each case the swelling sore?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By saying to these last, “Go farther back than your grandfather, and immediate ancestors, and you will find perchance many cooks, and drivers of asses, and shopkeepers:” but to the former, that are puffed up by the meanness of their forefathers, the contrary again; “And thou again, if thou proceed farther up among thy forefathers, wilt find many far more illustrious than thou art.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that nature hath this course, come let me prove it to thee even from the Scriptures.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Solomon was son of a king, and of an illustrious king, but that king’s father was one of the vile and ignoble.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And his grandfather on his mother’s side in like manner; for else he would not have given his daughter to a mere soldier.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wert to go up again higher from these mean persons, thou wilt see the race more illustrious and royal.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So in Saul’s case too, so in many others also, one shall come to this result.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then pride ourselves herein.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what is birth? tell me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nothing, but a name only without a substance; and this ye will know in that day.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But because that day is not yet come, let us now even from the things present persuade you, that hence arises no superiority.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For should war overtake us, should famine, should anything else, all these inflated conceits of noble birth are put to the proof: should disease, should pestilence come upon us, it knows not how to distinguish between the rich and the poor, the glorious and inglorious, the high born and him that is not such; neither doth death, nor the other reverses of fortune, but they all rise up alike against all; and if I may say something that is even marvellous, against the rich more of the two.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by how much they are less exercised in these things, so much the more do they perish, when overtaken by them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the fear too is greater with the rich.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For none so tremble at princes as they; and at multitudes, not less than at princes, yea rather much more; many such houses in fact have been subverted alike by the wrath of multitudes and the threatening of princes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the poor man is exempt from both these kinds of troubled waters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore let alone this nobility, and if thou wouldest show me that thou art noble, show the freedom of thy soul, such as that blessed man had (and he a poor man), who said to Herod, “It is not lawful for thee to have thy brother Philip’s wife;”such as he was possessed of, who before him was like him, and after him shall be so again; who said to Ahab, “I do not trouble Israel, but thou, and thy father’s house;”such as the prophets had, such as all the apostles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not like this are the souls of them that are slaves to wealth, but as they that are under ten thousand tutors, and taskmasters, so these dare not so much as lift up their eye, and speak boldly in behalf of virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the love of riches, and that of glory, and that of other things, looking terribly on them, make them slavish flatterers; there being nothing which so takes away liberty, as entanglement in worldly affairs, and the wearing what are accounted marks of distinction.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such an one hath not one master, nor two, nor three, but ten thousand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if ye would fain even number them, let us bring in some one of those that are in honor in kings’ courts, and let him have both very much wealth, and great power, and a birthplace excelling others, and distinction of ancestry, and let him be looked up to by all men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now then let us see, if this be not the very person to be more in slavery than all; and let us set in comparison with him, not a slave merely, but a slave’s slave, for many though servants have slaves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This slave’s slave then for his part hath but one master.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what though that one be not a freeman? yet he is but one, and the other looks only to his pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For albeit his master’s master seem to have power over him, yet for the present he obeys one only; and if matters between them two are well, he will abide in security all his life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But our man hath not one or two only, but many, and more grievous masters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And first he is in care about the sovereign himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it is not the same to have a mean person for a master, as to have a king, whose ears are buzzed into by many, and who becomes a property now to this set and now to that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Our man, though conscious of nothing, suspects all; both his comrades and his subordinates; both his friends and his enemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the other man too, you may say, fears his master.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But how is it the same thing, to have one or many, to make one timorous?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, if a man inquire carefully, he will not find so much as one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How, and in what sense?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas that slave hath no one that desires to put him out of that service of his, and to introduce himself (whence neither hath he any one to plot against him therein); these have not even any other pursuit, but to unsettle him that is more approved and more beloved by their ruler.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore also he must needs flatter all, his superiors, his equals, his friends.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For where envy is, and love of glory, there even sincere friendship has no strength.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as those of the same craft cannot love one another with a perfect and genuine love, so is it with rivals in honor also, and with them that long for the same among worldly objects.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence also great is the war within.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou what a swarm of masters, and of hard masters?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wilt thou that I show thee yet another, more grievous than this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They that are behind him, all of them strive to get before him: all that are before him, to hinder him from coming nearer them, and passing them by.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But O marvel!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I undertook indeed to show you masters, but our discourse, we find, coming on and waxing eager, hath performed more than my undertaking, pointing out foes instead of masters; or rather the same persons both as foes and as masters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For while they are courted like masters, they are terrible as foes, and they plot against us as enemies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When then any one hath the same persons both as masters, and as enemies, what can be worse than this calamity?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The slave indeed, though he be subject to command, yet nevertheless hath the advantage of care and good-will on the part of them who give him orders; but these, while they receive commands, are made enemies, and are set one against another; and that so much more grievously than those in battles, in that they both wound secretly, and in the mask of friends they treat men as their enemies would do, and oftentimes make themselves credit of the calamity of others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But not such are our circumstances; rather should another fare ill, there are many to grieve with him: should he obtain distinction, many to find pleasure with him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not so again the apostle: “For whether,” saith he, “one member suffer, all the members suffer with it; or one member be honored, all the members rejoice with it.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the words of him who gives these admonitions, are at one time, “What is my hope or joy? are not even ye?”at another, “Now we live, if ye stand fast in the Lord;”at another, “Out of much affliction and anguish of heart I wrote unto you;”and, “Who is weak, and I am not weak?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Who is offended, and I burn not?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then do we still endure the tempest and the billows of the world without, and not run to this calm haven, and leaving the names of good things, go on to the very things themselves?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For glory, and dignity, and wealth, and credit, and all such things, are names with them, but with us realities; just as the grievous things, death and dishonor and poverty, and whatever else is like them, are names indeed with us, but realities with them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And, if thou wilt, let us first bring forward glory, so lovely and desirable with all of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And I speak not of its being short-lived, and soon put out, but when it is in its bloom, then show it me.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Take not away the daubings and colored lines of the harlot, but bring her forward decked out, and exhibit her to us, for me thereupon to expose her deformity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Well then, of course thou wilt tell of her array, and her many lictors, and the heralds’ voice, and the listening of all classes, and the silence kept by the populace, and the blows given to all that come in one’s way, and the universal gazing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Are not these her splendors?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Come then, let us examine whether these things be not vain, and a mere unprofitable imagination.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wherein is the person we speak of the better for these things, either in body, or in soul? for this constitutes the man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Will he then be taller hereby, or stronger, or healthier, or swifter, or will he have his senses keener, and more piercing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, no one could say this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us go then to the soul, if haply we may find there any advantage occurring herefrom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Will such a one be more temperate, more gentle, more prudent, through that kind of attendance?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means, but rather quite the contrary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not as in the body, so also is the result here.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there the body indeed gains nothing in respect of its proper excellence; but here the mischief is not only the soul’s reaping no good fruit, but also its actually receiving much evil therefrom: hurried as it is by such means into haughtiness, and vainglory, and folly, and wrath, and ten thousand faults like them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But he rejoices,” thou wilt say, “and exults in these things, and they brighten him up.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The crowning pointof his evils lies in that word of thine, and the incurable part of the disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that rejoices in these things, would be unwilling however easily to be released from that which is the ground of his evils; yea, he hath blocked up against himself the way of healing by this delight.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that here most of all is the mischief, that he is not even pained, but rather rejoices, when the diseases are growing upon him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither is rejoicing always a good thing; since even thieves rejoice in stealing, and an adulterer in defiling his neighbor’s marriage bed, and the covetous in spoiling by violence, and the manslayer in murdering.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then look whether he rejoice, but whether it be for something profitable, lestperchance we find his joy to be such as that of the adulterer and the thief.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wherefore, tell me, doth he rejoice?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For his credit with the multitude, because he can puff himself up, and be gazed upon?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, what can be worse than this desire, and this ill-placed fondness? or if it be no bad thing, ye must leave off deriding the vainglorious and aspersing them with continual mockeries: ye must leave off uttering imprecations on the haughty and contemptuous.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But ye would not endure it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Well then, they too deserve plenty of censure, though they have plenty of lictors.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And all this I have said of the more tolerable sort of rulers; since the greater part of them we shall find transgressing more grievously than either robbers, or murderers, or adulterers, or spoilers of tombs, from not making a good use of their power.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed both their thefts are more shameless, and their butcheries more hardened, and their impurities far more enormous than the others; and they dig through, not one wall, but estates and houses without end, their prerogative making it very easy to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they serve a most grievous servitude, both stooping basely under their passions,and trembling at all their accomplices.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he only is free, and he only a ruler, and more kingly than all kings, who is delivered from his passions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowing then these things, let us follow after the true freedom, and deliver ourselves from the evil slavery, and let us account neither pomp of power nor dominion of wealth, nor any other such thing, to be blessed; but virtue only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thus shall we both enjoy security here, and attain unto the good things to come, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might, with the Father and the Holy Spirit, world without end.
